questions.question: What is the default Administrative Distance of a static route?
questions.explanation: Static routes have a default AD of 1, making them more trusted than any dynamic routing protocol but less trusted than directly connected routes (AD 0).

questions.question: Which command configures a static default route out interface G0/0 with next-hop 203.0.113.2?
questions.explanation: `ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 203.0.113.2` creates a default route — all netmask bits set to 0 means it matches every destination.

questions.question: What code appears in `show ip route` for a candidate default static route?
questions.explanation: `S*` marks a static route that is a candidate default route — the asterisk flags it as the potential gateway of last resort.

questions.question: Which static route acts as a backup to an OSPF route?
questions.explanation: Adding `111` at the end sets the AD to 111 — higher than OSPF's 110. This makes it a floating static route that only installs if the OSPF route fails.

questions.question: What is a host route?
options: A route to a single specific host using a /32 mask
A route used only by end hosts
A route to a remote subnet
questions.explanation: A host route uses a /32 mask, pointing to a single specific IP. Network routes use mask lengths shorter than /32.

questions.question: What is two-way reachability, and why does it matter for static routes?
options: Both routers must use the same routing protocol
Both directions must have matching routes or replies will fail
Both interfaces must be in the same VLAN
Both hosts must have the same subnet mask
questions.explanation: Traffic must be able to reach the destination AND return. Forgetting the return route is a classic static routing mistake — pings fail because replies can't find their way back.

questions.question: Which command tests a Layer 3 path hop-by-hop from a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: `traceroute` is the Cisco IOS command. `tracert` is the Windows equivalent. Both trace the Layer 3 path to a destination.

questions.question: Why is a loopback interface useful in lab static-routing scenarios?
options: It is always up/up unless shut down, perfect for simulating remote networks
It routes packets faster than physical interfaces
It is the only interface OSPF runs on
questions.explanation: Loopback interfaces are virtual and always up/up unless manually shut down. They're ideal for simulating remote networks in labs without needing extra hardware.

questions.question: On a serial link, which side sets the clock rate?
options: Whichever side has the higher IP
questions.explanation: On serial links, the DCE side sets the clock rate (e.g., `clock rate 64000`); the DTE side receives the clock signal.

questions.question: What does the static route `ip route 192.168.1.0 255.255.255.0 G0/0` specify?
questions.explanation: This command specifies only an exit interface with no next-hop IP. Works reliably on point-to-point links; on multi-access segments it relies on Proxy ARP.

questions.question: What is the result of configuring two static routes to the same destination with equal AD and no metric differences?
options: Only one is installed; the other is ignored
Both routes install and ECMP load-balances between them
The newer command overwrites the older
questions.explanation: Two equal-cost static routes create ECMP — the router installs both and load-balances traffic between them.

questions.question: Which command shows only the static routes in the routing table?
questions.explanation: `show ip route static` filters the routing table to display only routes with code S (static). Similar filters exist for `connected`, `ospf`, `eigrp`, etc.
